title: Volunteering and Giving Back to the Community
prompt: Talk about the value of volunteering and community involvement. Have you ever volunteered or helped your community in some way? Describe your experience or explain why you would or would not like to volunteer. What kinds of volunteering do you think are most valuable, and why?

examples.title: Helping Others and Helping Myself
examples.body:
I think volunteering is a very good thing to do, and I have had some personal experience with it. A couple of years ago, I volunteered at a local community centre that helped elderly people. My job was to visit some of the older residents once a week and have a conversation with them, help them with small tasks, or just keep them company.

At first, I wasn't sure what to expect. I was a little nervous because I didn't know what to say. But I quickly realised that most of the people were just happy to have someone to talk to. It made me feel very good to know that such a simple thing — just listening and being present — could make a big difference to someone's day.

I also think I learned a lot from the experience. I heard many interesting stories and I developed more patience and empathy. I think volunteering teaches you skills that you can't easily learn in a classroom.

I believe the most valuable kinds of volunteering are those that address real needs in the community — things like helping the homeless, supporting people with mental health difficulties, or working with children who need extra support. These areas often don't receive enough government funding, so volunteers are really important.

Some people say they don't have time to volunteer, and I understand that life can be very busy. But even a few hours a month can make a difference. I think if more people volunteered, communities would be much stronger and more connected. And personally, I always felt better after my volunteering sessions than before — it is genuinely good for you too.

examples.title: The Reciprocity of Service
examples.body:
My experience with volunteering began somewhat accidentally. I signed up for a programme working with newly arrived refugees — helping with language practice, administrative tasks, and general orientation to a new city — largely because a colleague recommended it and I had a free Saturday. I expected it to be worthwhile in a vague, good-citizenship sense. I didn't expect it to be one of the more formative experiences of the past few years.

What struck me immediately was how asymmetric the conventional narrative of volunteering is. We tend to frame it in terms of giving — the volunteer gives time, skill, or energy to someone who needs it. That framing is accurate as far as it goes, but it obscures how much flows in the other direction. The people I worked with had navigated circumstances of extraordinary complexity — bureaucratic, linguistic, emotional — and their resilience and resourcefulness were things I found genuinely instructive. I came away from those sessions having learned at least as much as I'd contributed.

I think this reciprocal quality is worth taking seriously when we discuss volunteering, because I suspect it's what sustains people's involvement over time. Pure altruism is a fragile motivator; the sense that an activity is genuinely enriching — that it connects you to people and experiences you wouldn't otherwise encounter — is far more durable.

In terms of which forms of volunteering are most valuable, I'm sceptical of easy hierarchies, but I do think the most impactful tends to involve sustained, skilled engagement rather than episodic participation. Working consistently with a local organisation over months — bringing specific expertise, maintaining relationships, understanding context — almost certainly achieves more than a single large-scale effort. It also demands more honesty about what you're actually able to offer.

I'd push back slightly on the framing of volunteering as simply giving back, because I think it implies a clarity about the direction of benefit that the reality rarely supports. At its best, it's a relationship — unequal in some respects, but genuinely mutual in others — and acknowledging that makes it both more honest and more sustainable.
